content:
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="perturbetur_de_1">
							, <gloss>is alarmed for.</gloss>
								<milestone n="171.5" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="novi_judici_1">,
							i.e. the special tribunal established by vote of the people to try all
							cases arising out of the brawl between Milo and Clodius. On this
							occasion the court was surrounded, not by the usual throng of
							spectators, but by an armed guard (see Introd., p. 170 of text), — hence <foreign xml:lang="lat">nova forma</foreign>. <milestone n="171.7" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="requirunt_1">
							, <gloss>miss, seek in vain.</gloss>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pro_templis_2">:
							see plan of Forum. The guards held the entrances to these temples as
							important strategic points for defence against the mob. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_aliquid_2">
							, <gloss>do not fail to bring something</gloss> (of terror or
							constraint). <milestone n="171.11" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_possimus_2">
							, <gloss>so that we cannot even be relieved of fear</gloss> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">non timere</foreign>) <gloss>without some fear</gloss>
							.
</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="foro_2">, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="judicio_2">:
							such a display of arms in places especially devoted to occupations of
							peace must necessarily cause some uneasiness. <pb n="429"/>
								<milestone n="171.14" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_putarem_2">:
							Cicero artfully assumes throughout the oration that the authorities
							(including Pompey) are on the side of Milo. <milestone n="171.16" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="me_recreat_2">:
							to preserve the emphatic position we may change the voice, —<gloss>I am
							reassured</gloss>; so </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="reficit_2">, <gloss>I
							am revived</gloss>. <milestone n="171.17" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="justitiae_suae_2">:
							§ <ref n="AG 343">343</ref>, c (214, d); cf. B. 198, 3; cf. G. 366;
							cf. H. 439 (401); H.-B. 340; 50 <foreign xml:lang="lat">sapientiae</foreign>
							in 1.19. <milestone n="171.18" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="putaret_2">, <gloss>would
							not have thought</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 446">446</ref> (311, a); B.
							280, 4; G. 258; H. 552, 554, 2, 3 (485 and N.1); H.-B. 519, 2. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tradidisset_2">,
							subj. of integral part. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illa_arma_3">,
							etc.: on the first day of the trial, when M. Marcellus began to
							cross-examine one of the witnesses against Milo, he was so terrified by
							the rush of the mob that he took refuge on the <foreign xml:lang="lat">
							praetor</foreign>'s bench. Pompey, alarmed by the same disturbance, came
							down next day with an armed guard, and the trial was allowed to proceed
							without interruption. <milestone n="171.23" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quieto_3">,
							etc.: i.e. not only to be undisturbed but to be greatly encouraged. <milestone n="171.25" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quae_civium_3">
							, <gloss>so far at least as it consists of citizens</gloss> (covertly
							suggesting that the supporters of Clodius were not citizens, but
							gladiators and the like). <milestone n="171.26" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="neque_non_3">,
							etc.: notice the double negative, — <gloss>there is no one... who does
							not</gloss>, etc. <milestone n="171.30" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="decertari_3">
							(impersonal), <gloss>that the conflict is</gloss>. Here Cicero suggests
							what one of the chief points of his defence is to be, — that Milo had
							always acted in defence of the state. <milestone n="172.1" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eorum_3">, <gloss>namely,
							of those</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 344">344</ref> (214, e); B. 197; G.
							368, a.; cf. H. 441 (397, I); H.-B. 349. <milestone n="172.3" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hesterna_contione_3">
							, <gloss>yesterday's harangue</gloss>. The day before, after the court
							adjourned, one T. Munatius Plancus (see sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 12">12</ref>)
							had harangued the crowd, urging them to be on hand next day and not
							suffer Milo to escape. On this day, the last of the trial, shops were
							closed throughout the city; Pompey posted guards in the Forum and all
							its approaches; he himself sat, as on the day before, in front of the
							Treasury, girt with a select body of troops. When Cicero began to speak,
							"he was received by an outcry of the party of Clodius, who could not be
							restrained even by fear of the surrounding soldiery." <milestone n="172.4" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="judicaretis_3">:
							§ <ref n="AG 444">444</ref> (268); B. 277; G. 259; H. 557 (486, ii);
							H.-B. 503. <milestone n="172.6" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="retineatis_3">:
							the penalty for the offence with which Milo was charged was banishment,
							by which he would lose his rights as citizen. <pb n="430"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="locus_4">, <gloss>
							opportunity</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="amplissimorum_ordinum_4">:
							the court was made up of Senators, <foreign xml:lang="lat">equites</foreign>,
							and <foreign xml:lang="lat">tribuni aerarii</foreign>. <milestone n="172.12" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="delectis_4">:
							the whole body of jurors (360) was selected, though the particular jury
							(of 51) was drawn by lot. <milestone n="172.14" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="re_sententus_4">
							, <gloss>by act and verdict</gloss>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nobis_duobus_5">
							, <gloss>than we two</gloss>, i.e. the orator and his client. Cicero
							constantly associates himself with Milo in this fashion, thus not merely
							following the custom of advocates, but also representing Milo as engaged
							in the same kind of opposition to the dangerous elements in the state as
							that which had made his own consulship illustrious. <milestone n="172.23" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="crudelissimorum_5">:
							exile was the worst that Milo had to fear. Here Cicero alludes to his
							own experience of it, which had resulted from his patriotic efforts
							against the Catilinarian conspirators. <milestone n="172.24" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ceteras_5">,
							etc.: it was to be expected that a politician should undergo abuse and
							even illegal violence in the stormy public life of the time, but such
							dangers were not to be anticipated in an impartial court. <milestone n="172.27" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ex_ordinibus_5">:
							see note on <foreign xml:lang="lat">ordinum</foreign>, sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 4">4</ref> (<ref n="pl172.11">p. 172, l. 11</ref>). <milestone n="172.29" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="salutem_5">,
							i.e. not his personal safety in the modern sense, but his political
							rights (see note on 1.23, above). <milestone n="172.30" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="talis_viros_5">:
							it was admitted, says Asconius, that no body of jurors had ever been
							more illustrious or just than those who composed this court. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quamquam_6">
							, <gloss>and yet</gloss> (corrective). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tribunatu_6">:
							in the year <date when="-0057">B.C. 57</date> Milo, as tribune, had
							materially assisted in procuring the recall of Cicero from banishment.
							It was partly gratitude for this service that led Cicero to undertake
							Milo's defence. <milestone n="173.3" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="abutemur_6">
							, <gloss>take unfair advantage of</gloss> Cicero declines to use Milo's
							services to the state as an argument in his favor, and boldly declares
							that he will put the case on the bare facts, contending that Milo acted
							in self-defence. But in the very act of declining to dwell on these
							services he subtly emphasizes them, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fuerit_7">,
							subj. of integral part. <milestone n="173.13" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_orationem_7">
							, <gloss>to that line of argument</gloss>
								<milestone n="173.14" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="videntur_7">,
							etc.: § <ref n="AG 582">582</ref> (330, b, 1); B. 332, b; G. 528, R.2;
							H. 611, N.1, 2 (534, i, N.1) cf. H.-B. 590, I and a. <milestone n="173.20" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="primum_7">,
							i.e. the first capital trial that occurred in Rome. <milestone n="173.21" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="M_Horati_7">:
							the famous story of the three Horatii and the three Curiatii. When
							Horatius was condemned to death for the murder of his sister, he was
							acquitted on appeal to the people; and this incident passed as the
							origin of <foreign xml:lang="lat">provocatio</foreign>, or appeal to the
							people from the decision of a magistrate. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nondum_libera_7">,
							i.e. under the kings: it was in the reign of Tullus Hostilius, <date when="-0668">B.C. 668</date>. <milestone n="173.22" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="comitiis_7">,
							i.e. by the vote of the Roman people assembled to decide the appeal. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="P_Africanum_8">,
							i.e. <foreign xml:lang="lat">Aemilianus</foreign>: he was cousin (by
							adoption) and brother-in-law of Gracchus, and friendly to the spirit of
							his reforms, although not sympathizing with his violent course. <milestone n="173.27" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="C_Carbone_8">:
							C. Carbo was an unscrupulous politician who supported Ti. Gracchus, but
							afterwards was a bitter antagonist of C. Gracchus. <pb n="432"/>
								<milestone n="173.30" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ahala_8">,
							etc.: these are cases which would have to be called infamous murders
							unless the principle that homicide is sometimes justifiable were
							admitted. The instances referred to were stock examples in Roman oratory
							(see Cat. 1, sects. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 3">3</ref>, <ref n="Cic. Mil. 4">4</ref>), though, in fact, Ahala and the others
							were all driven into exile by a reaction of popular feeling. <milestone n="173.31" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="senatus_8">:
							because the execution of the Catilinarian conspirators was by vote of
							the Senate. <milestone n="174.2" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fictis_fabulis_8">,
							properly, <gloss>mythical dramas</gloss>: the reference is to the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Eumenides</foreign> of Aeschylus, which treats of the
							expiation of the guilt of Orestes, son of Agamemnon, at the court of
							Areopagus in Athens. When six judges had pronounced for condemnation and
							six for acquittal, Pallas gave her casting vote for mercy. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="doctissimi_8">,
							i.e. the greatest poets. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="duodecim_tabulae_9">:
							the "Twelve Tables" were the code which formed the basis of Roman law,
							drawn up <date when="-0451">B.C. 451</date> by an elected board of ten
							commissioners (<foreign xml:lang="lat">decemviri</foreign>). This
							codification of the laws continued in force, and was the starting point
							of the legal education of every Roman, and of all later development of
							Roman law. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quoquo_modo_9">
							, <gloss>under any circumstances</gloss>. <milestone n="174.8" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quoquo_modo_9_2">
							, <gloss>in whatever way</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quis_9">
							(indefinite), <gloss>one</gloss>; the preceding <foreign xml:lang="lat">
							quis</foreign> is interrogative. <milestone n="174.13" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vi_defenditur_9">
							, <gloss>violence offered is repelled by force. </gloss>-<foreign xml:lang="lat">pudicitiam</foreign> . . . <foreign xml:lang="lat">
							eriperet</foreign>, <gloss>tried to rob of his honor</gloss>. <milestone n="174.14" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tribunus_9">:
							C. Lusius, son of Marius's sister. This was a stock instance among
							rhetoricians in arguing the just limits of self-defence. <milestone n="174.17" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="scelere_solutum_9">
							, <gloss>acquitted of guilt</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vero_10">:
							introducing, as usual, a strong case. <milestone n="174.19" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="comitatus_10">
							, <gloss>body-guards</gloss>, which would seem to have been a common
							thing among these gentlemen of Rome, as among the partisan chieftains of
							the Middle Ages. <milestone n="174.20" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="volunt_10">, <gloss>
							mean</gloss>. <milestone n="174.20" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adripuimus_10">
							, <gloss>caught</gloss>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hausimus_10">
							, <gloss>imbibed</gloss>; <foreign xml:lang="lat">expressimus</foreign>, <gloss>wrought
							out</gloss>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="imbuti_10">, <gloss>
							steeped</gloss>. <milestone n="175.1" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_esset_10">,
							purpose clause after <foreign xml:lang="lat">lex</foreign> (a word of
							decreeing). <milestone n="175.2" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="incidisset_10">:
							in the words of the <foreign xml:lang="lat">lex</foreign>, - <foreign xml:lang="lat">incident</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sflent_11">:
							notice the emphatic position. <milestone n="175.4" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="velit_11">:
							subjunctive because of sit. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ante_quam_11">:
							§ <ref n="AG 434">434</ref> (262); G. 574, R.1; H. (p.293, footnote
							1); cf. 14.-B. 507, 4, footnote 1 <milestone n="175.5" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="etsi_11">,
							i.e. there is no need for me to appeal to the law of nature to establish
							the right to kill in self-defence, for this right is established <pb n="433"/> by the judicial interpretation of a particular statute
							(of Sulla). This statute (<foreign xml:lang="lat">de sicariis</foreign>)
							dealt with murder in general and went so far as to provide a penalty for
							the carrying of a weapon with intent to kill. The courts had ruled, says
							Cicero, that, under this law, a man who carried a weapon for
							self-defence was not carrying it <foreign xml:lang="lat">hominis
							occidendi causa</foreign>, in the meaning of the statute. <milestone n="175.10" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="judicaretur_11">:
							the subject is antecedent of <foreign xml:lang="lat">qui</foreign>. <milestone n="175.11" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hoc_causa_11">
							, <gloss>let this point stand as the law of the case</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sequitur_illud_12">
							, <gloss>the next point is this</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 597">597</ref>,
							d, i (344, d, I); Cf. H.-B. 625. <milestone n="175.16" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="contra_factam_12">:
							a technical phrase, amounting to "unlawful (unconstitutional) violence";
							cf. our "a breach of the peace." </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illam_12">,
							i.e. <foreign xml:lang="lat">caedem</foreign>. <milestone n="175.17" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sententiis_12">,
							.e. its expressed vote; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="studiis_12">,
							i.e. the interest it displayed in Milo's behalf? <milestone n="175.19" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nec_tacitis_12">
							, <gloss>loudly</gloss>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nec_occultis_12">
							, <gloss>in plain terms</gloss>. <milestone n="176.1" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="declarant_12">:
							to preserve the emphasis we may change the voice, — <gloss>it is shown
							by</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hujus_tribuni_12">:
							the body of Clodius, left in the highway, had been picked up and sent to
							Rome, where its wounds were exposed to public gaze till, in the fury of
							the time, it was dragged to the Senate-house. Here a funeral-pile was
							made of desks, benches, and other furniture, and in the conflagration
							the Senate-house itself, with several other buildings, was destroyed.
							The tribune T. Munatius Plancus, who incited the mob to burn the body,
							is called <foreign xml:lang="lat">ambustus</foreign>, <gloss>
							fire-scorched</gloss>, because his influence suffered in the reaction of
							public feeling which followed the unintended conflagration; hence, too,
							his <gloss>harangues</gloss> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">contiones</foreign>)
							are referred to as <gloss>still-born</gloss> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">
							intermortuae</foreign>). <milestone n="176.2" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="potentiam_12">
							, <gloss>unlawful domination</gloss>. <milestone n="176.6" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="officiosos_12">
							, <gloss>serviceable</gloss>, in the way of forensic advocacy. <milestone n="176.8" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sane_12">, <gloss>if
							you like; or for aught I care</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vero_13">: in
							reference to the statement at the beginning of sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 12">12</ref>. </gloss>

							himself in female attire. The mysteries were being held at the official
							residence of Caesar, then <foreign xml:lang="lat">pontifex maximus</foreign>,
							and the latter's wife Pompeia was thought to have connived at the
							intrusion. Caesar affected to believe no harm, but presently divorced

							suspicion." Being brought to trial, Clodius tried to prove an <foreign xml:lang="lat">alibi</foreign>, but this attempt was frustrated, in

							he never forgave Cicero for appearing as a witness against him. <milestone n="176.15" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="divisa_est_14">

							disturbances referred to, was discussed in the Senate, a resolution
							opposed to it was offered. This consisted of two clauses: (I) that the
							disturbances were <foreign xml:lang="lat">contra rem publicam</foreign>,
							and (2) that cases arising out of them should be brought in the regular
							courts, but should be advanced on the docket (<foreign xml:lang="lat">
							veteribus</foreign> —<foreign xml:lang="lat">legibus</foreign>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">extra ordinem</foreign>). The tribune Q. Fufius
							Calenus (whom Cicero refuses to name: <foreign xml:lang="lat">nescio quo</foreign>)
							demanded that the question be divided. The first clause was then passed,
							whereupon the tribune Plancus interposed his veto (<foreign xml:lang="lat">empta intercessione</foreign>, l.10) and prevented
							the second from being voted upon. Thus it was made to appear that the
							Senate approved the new tribunal, and Pompey's law was immediately
							passed by the public assembly, while the more cautious action of the
							Senate (<foreign xml:lang="lat">reliqua auctoritas</foreign>) was
							blocked by the <foreign xml:lang="lat">intercessio</foreign> of Plancus. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dubitaret_petenda_81">;

							conditional sentence consisting of a cont. to fact apod. (<foreign xml:lang="lat">dubitaret</foreign>) with a prot. "if he had done it"
							implied. The argument is as follows: Milo does not deny killing Clodius
							in <gloss>self-defence</gloss>, which is only an <gloss>excuse</gloss>
							for the homicide; if, therefore, he had killed him <gloss>deliberately</gloss>,
							would he not gladly admit it, since he might justly expect such a
							service to the state as the removal of this desperado to meet with
							gratitude from all? Hence Milo's denial is worthy of credence, since it
							would be for his interest to admit the charge. <milestone n="203.20" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dictator_103">:
